summary:
The Ashanti Empire (Asante Confederacy, c. 1701–1902 AD) was the most powerful state in West Africa during the 18th and 19th centuries, centered in the forest zone of modern-day Ghana, whose sophisticated political institutions, military prowess, and cultural achievements made it the principal indigenous power resisting British colonial expansion in West Africa. The Confederacy was founded around 1701 by Osei Tutu, who unified the Akan-speaking Asante clans by creating a potent founding myth: the Golden Stool (Sika Dwa Kofi) — a wooden stool overlaid in gold that appeared from the heavens and contained the sunsum (spirit and strength) of the Asante nation — which served as the sacred symbol of Asante unity that could never be sat upon or surrendered.

The Ashanti state generated enormous wealth through control of the gold trade (Ghana means 'Warrior King' but the region was known to Europeans as the Gold Coast for its gold deposits) and its participation in the [[event:2341|Atlantic slave trade]], supplying enslaved people to European ships in exchange for firearms and manufactured goods. Gold funded an elaborate royal court at Kumasi, sophisticated bronze casting (the Asante goldweight tradition), fine kente cloth weaving, and a professional army. The Ashanti expanded aggressively throughout the 18th century, defeating neighboring states and reaching the coast. A series of Anglo-Ashanti Wars (1823, 1863, 1874, 1896, 1900) marked the long struggle against British colonialism; the British sacked and burned Kumasi in 1874 and again in 1900, when the famous 'War of the Golden Stool' broke out after the British Governor demanded to sit on the sacred stool. The Confederacy was formally annexed as the Gold Coast Colony in 1902.
